course_description:
This course examines current theory and research in learning and the implications of this work for teaching and learning in educational contexts. It looks at ways for teachers to enhance both their own learning as well as the learning of their students. This course may not be offered in person if the enrolment is less than 10 students.

This course explores issues related to human learning in a variety of contexts including schools, homes, universities and the community. Students will be provided with opportunities to examine the processes of thinking and learning. Attention will be given to individuals with developmental disabilities, those with learning difficulties and others who find thinking and/or learning challenging. ﾠ

restrictions: Restricted to students enrolled in the GCEdSt and MEdSt
Another instance of the same course
Separate 2-hour seminars for In-Person and External classes

course_contacts.name: Dr Holly Chen
course_contacts.notes: Office: Social Sciences Building (Building 24), Room 610 Email is the best way to contact me. You are also welcome to email me to schedule a meeting at the campus or online.

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
The main components of the course are the 2-hour weekly seminars, readings, activities, videos and extra materials in the Course Resources section on the Blackboard. Regular access and engagement with the weekly sessions is very important to optimise your learning. Students enrolled in the In-Person class are required to attend the class at campus, and the External class is offered on Zoom. Please check the timetable information for the location of the venue. Feel free to contact the Lecturer if you have any queries.
Recording of Seminars: Please be aware that teaching at UQ will be recorded for the benefit of student learning. If you would prefer not to be captured either by voice or image, please advise your course coordinator before class so accommodations can be made. For further information see PPL3.20.06 Recording of Teaching at UQ. The audio recording of the 2-hour seminars will be uploaded by the university on the Blackboard after class each week. Please note there will be periods on the audio when the students will be discussing or participating in group activities.
course_aims: The course aims to introduce students to an understanding of the processes of thinking and learning. It also aims to both build a comprehensive understanding of learning from various theoretical points of view and to consider the application and/or implications of the understanding for a range of settings and individuals.

learning_outcomes.description: understand the process of human thinking and learning from both individual cognitive and sociocultural perspectives,
understand the cognitive processes of individuals with developmental disabilities, those with learning difficulties and others who find thinking and/or learning challenging;
apply your understanding of thinking and learning to develop more effective programs and instruction in classrooms and other educational settings;
summarise, analyse, explain and evaluate theory and/or research and use contemporary literature;
develop, organise and sustain logical and well-reasoned argument(s); and
demonstrate clear, fluent and coherent communication skills consistent with professional and academic conventions.

assessment_details.title: Assessment Task 1: Reflective Learning Journals Entry One
assessment_details.task_description: Length: 1800 words, ±10% leeway is allowed, including in-text references. References at the end are not counted towards the word count. Assessment Description: To establish your developing, understanding and application of the course content, you are required to write reflective learning journals by responding to the listed questions for each entry. These questions are set in alignment with weekly topics which require you to complete on a weekly basis. Your reflective learning journals will be organised into two entries for submission. Entry One (Assessment Task 1) are related to the content learnt in Topics 1 − 5, and Entry Two (Assessment Task 3) are related to Topics 6−10 (and review of previous Topics). Each entry should be informed by the seminar and course readings to demonstrate your capacity to engage with and reflect on the key ideas of the learning materials. These entries will help you develop a metacognitive approach to learning and connect with the teaching and learning context. They will also provide you with an opportunity to gain more feedback that is formative and prepare for the following assessment task. You are expected to engage with course readings and other quality literature as required in each question. A compiled reference list must be provided at the end of the submission, which is not included in the word limit. See the detail of Task Description (including the questions of each Entry), Guidelines and Marking Criteria on the course Blackboard. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The assignment is to be submitted electronically to a Turnitin link on the course Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Assessment Task 2: Application in Lesson Plan Development and Interactive Oral Presentation
assessment_details.weight: 50% Written submission (1500 words), and oral presentation (7min, including 2 min Q&A)
assessment_details.due_date: 24/04/2026 2:00 pm Your oral presentation will be individually scheduled beforehand and conducted in class within two weeks after the written submission date.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Length: Written submission: 1500 words (±10% leeway, including in-text references. References at the end are not counted towards the word count.) and interactive oral presentation: 7 minutes in total including Q&A. Assessment Topic: There are a range of cognitive challenges or difficulties that impact the learning experienced by students with learning difficulties/learning disabilities*(as understood in Australia and the United States) at primary or secondary schools or even in tertiary education. Given what you have learnt in this course and building from the Reflective Learning Journals, create a lesson plan or a series of lessons in a curriculum area of your choice at primary or secondary levels, or for tertiary education. To optimise students’ learning, your lesson design, teaching and assessment strategies need to be responsive to four aspects of cognitive challenges or difficulties that students with learning difficulties/learning disabilities often encounter, choosing from what has been addressed in the course such as attention, working memory, remembering, knowledge building, problem-solving, metacognition. In this task, you will need to include the following: Part 1: Written Component (60%): ● A lesson plan or a series of lessons (700 words): Explain the context of the lesson plan including the teaching subject, topic, a firm learning goal(s), year level, positioned phase of the lesson, prior learning, and the four chosen aspects of cognitive difficulties to be focused on. This is to provide a background for the lesson. Then clearly describe the lesson structure including a sequence of activities students will do, duration, resources and what support you will provide. You can use a table template for the lesson plan. ● Explanations and justifications of lesson design and strategies used (800 words): - Provide an overall statement of your lesson design and teaching approaches and what theoretical perspectives support your planning. - Explain and justify why the specific design features, activities and/or strategies will be effective in supporting students with learning difficulties/learning disabilities in response to the 4 aspects of cognitive difficulties, based on evidence from recent relevant academic and/or research literature, including course reading. ● References (Included at the end, not counted in the word count) ● Word count and acknowledgement of use (or not use) of AI (Included on the first page, not counted in the word count) Part 2: Interactive Oral Presentation in Class (40%, 7 min in total including 5 min presentation and 2 min Q&A): School teachers often work as a team in curriculum design in real-world contexts. You are invited to share your lesson plan with your colleagues. In the 5-minute presentation in the class, you will describe to your colleagues your lesson plan and justification for your lesson design, activities and strategies to mitigate the cognitive difficulties of students with learning difficulties/learning disabilities and support their learning. You will be expected to explain your lesson plan and the rationale for it based on the research, theory and content covered in the course and respond to the questions from the audience. The questions will be drawn upon the written component as well as presentation. Ensure you demonstrate a professional and collegial communication style, within the time limit. It is strongly suggested that you use visuals to support your communication, by considering the use of PowerPoint slides (no more than 4 slides) or a small, printed handout (no more than 200 words) e.g. a summary of key points discussed, a table of the lesson structure for colleagues. See the detail of Task Description, Guidelines and Marking Criteria on the course Blackboard. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The written component is to be submitted electronically to a Turnitin link on the course Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Application for Extension
Your application for an extension for Part 1: Written component must be lodged via myRequests at myUQ only, following all policy requirements. If an extension is required for Part 2: Interactive Oral Presentation you must contact the Course Coordinator directly to discuss rescheduling.
Late submission
A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
